Q: Used e-GMAT as the main prep course and completed it nearly twice after the first interrupted pass lost rhythm.
Recognized that returning to academics after 12 years required rebuilding fundamentals from scratch.
Used Quant improvement to support DI improvement.
Aimed for roughly 90% accuracy on medium questions and 70% accuracy on hard questions for 645-plus-level scoring.
V: Started with weak Verbal fundamentals and rebuilt from sentence-structure basics.
Learned CR question types systematically, including assumption, strengthen, and weaken distinctions.
Improved by deliberately understanding how to approach each Verbal question type.
Used regular practice to stay familiar with question patterns even when doing fewer questions.
Used GMAT Ninja videos for some Verbal question types.
Preferred mental notes while reading, using scratchpad ABCDE elimination marks rather than heavy note-taking.
DI: Focused DI practice on Graphs and Table Analysis.
Practiced quickly reading, gathering, and accurately extracting data from graphs under timed conditions.
Used pattern recognition from practice as the main DI lever.
Warned that raw number of incorrect DI questions does not map cleanly to score; missing easy questions hurts more.
Used GMAT Club sectional tests toward the end for additional practice.
overall: Prepared after 12 years away from academics and scored 655 after a long, uneven journey.
Used e-GMAT, official mocks 1-6, and GMAT Club sectional tests.
Learned not to rush through lessons after a long break, because shortcutting basics only extended the process.
Made error logging the central lesson: log mistakes from day one and schedule review time at least twice weekly.
Used error logs to understand why answers were wrong and which concepts needed review.
Accepted doing fewer new questions in exchange for actually learning from mistakes.

Q: Brushed up Quant with mba.com free practice tests and GMAT Club before the first attempt.
After running out of time near Quant question 19, kept going and relied on later sections to compensate.
Used official practice tests 1-4 and GMAT Club explanations for missed questions.
V: Used AnalystPrep YouTube videos for structured approaches to Verbal question types.
Identified Critical Reasoning Analysis/Critique questions as a weakness after reviewing practice and official results.
Used GMAT Club explanations for questions missed in practice tests.
DI: Identified math-related Data Sufficiency as a major weakness after the first official attempt.
For DS, used AnalystPrep's strategy of listing the metrics needed and the metrics required to find those.
Bought official practice tests 3 and 4 for more exposure to question types.
Used AnalystPrep YouTube videos for structured DI strategies.
overall: Moved from a 695 first attempt to 725 after diagnosing weak question types and adding structure.
Learned that a high cold practice result can create overconfidence and underpreparation.
Gathered practice-exam and official-attempt results, then listed the percentage wrong by question type.
Used official practice tests, GMAT Club explanations, and AnalystPrep YouTube as the core resources.
Saw the proctored environment as more mistake-prone than practice and therefore needing a methodical approach.
Stayed engaged after a weak Quant section rather than giving up on the rest of the test.

Q: Used official books and online question banks as the most important resources.
Used online question banks as pseudo-tests by doing 20, 21, or 23 questions in 45 minutes.
Used TTP's five-day free trial only for unfamiliar Probability and Combinatorics topics.
Followed semi-topical Quant study after a general review, rebuilding math after dropping it years earlier.
After early mock weakness, did two weeks of intense Quant practice.
Learned not to ignore geometry entirely after seeing an unexpected similarity-of-triangles question on test day.
V: Skimmed Manhattan and PowerScore Verbal books but relied mostly on existing Verbal strength.
Used official and unofficial mocks to experiment with mindset and section order.
Took more time in official Verbal because the section felt harder than usual, despite normally finishing early.
Changed two marked Verbal answers from correct to incorrect, reinforcing caution with review changes.
DI: Treated DI as mostly practice once math and Verbal concepts were in place.
Used official content and GMAT Club mocks for DI practice.
Benefited from six DS questions in a row on test day because DS was fast for the author.
Found TPA easy and MSR confusing but manageable.
Finished DI early with time to double-check and got only one DI question wrong.
overall: Prepared in two months for a single-attempt 755 while avoiding paid unofficial resources.
Studied three to four hours per day, five days per week in month one and every day in month two.
Kept sleep and social activity in the routine so studying did not become a burden.
Used official mocks as the main score guide and unofficial mocks mainly for stamina building.
Retook official mocks and treated the official mock pool as enough practice when used carefully.
Built a detailed error log that analyzed conceptual holes, thought process, 50-50 misses, instincts, and reflex errors.
Used mocks to experiment with section order and test mindset before trying anything on the real exam.
Did not study heavily the day before; reviewed prior mock mistakes, rested, and used a short cross-section warm-up on test morning.
Aimed to practice hard enough that even an unlucky test day would still be good enough.
